name: Strecker Memorandum
description: The Strecker Memorandum is a pseudoscientific document circulating primarily within conspiracy theory circles, particularly those concerned with the origins of AIDS. It alleges that AIDS was a man-made disease deliberately introduced into the population, often specifying that it was created by the National Institutes of Health (NIH) and the World Health Organization (WHO). This claim is based on interpretations of research conducted by Dr. Robert Strecker and his brother, Theodore Strecker. Proponents argue that the Strecker Memorandum provides evidence of a global conspiracy to depopulate the world or target specific groups. The theory gained traction in the 1980s and 1990s, fueled by distrust of government institutions and the lack of understanding surrounding the early stages of the AIDS epidemic. It often overlaps with other conspiracy theories, such as those involving secret societies and eugenics programs. Mainstream scientific and medical communities overwhelmingly reject the claims of the Strecker Memorandum. Extensive research has demonstrated that HIV, the virus that causes AIDS, originated in non-human primates and crossed over to humans naturally, likely through the consumption of bushmeat. No credible scientific evidence supports the claim that AIDS was created in a laboratory or intentionally released. The Strecker Memorandum is considered a dangerous piece of misinformation that has contributed to stigma, discrimination, and distrust of public health initiatives. Its persistence highlights the challenges of combating conspiracy theories, particularly those that exploit fear and uncertainty surrounding complex scientific issues. The document continues to be cited in online forums and alternative media, despite its lack of scientific validity.
subtitle: Alleged AIDS origins conspiracy theory.

claims.claim: AIDS was created in a laboratory by the NIH/WHO.
claims.evidence: Interpretation of research by Dr. Robert Strecker, particularly enzymatic arguments about HIV's origin.
claims.criticism: Overwhelming scientific consensus points to natural zoonotic transmission from primates to humans. No credible evidence supports laboratory creation.

claims.claim: AIDS was intentionally released to depopulate the world or target specific groups.
claims.evidence: Circumstantial evidence and interpretations of government documents often related to population control.
claims.criticism: Lack of concrete evidence. The theory relies on misinterpretations and selective use of information. Genetic analysis of HIV strains contradicts the idea of a deliberate release.

claims.claim: The Strecker Memorandum provides proof of a global conspiracy.
claims.evidence: The memorandum itself, along with supposed inconsistencies in official narratives about AIDS origins.
claims.criticism: The memorandum's claims are based on flawed scientific reasoning and have been thoroughly debunked by experts in virology, epidemiology, and medicine.

keyFigures.name: Robert Strecker
keyFigures.role: Proponent and author of the Strecker Memorandum

keyFigures.name: Theodore Strecker
keyFigures.role: Brother of Robert, involved in the initial research and promotion of the theory

keyFigures.name: Luc Montagnier
keyFigures.role: While initially credited by some proponents, Montagnier's later statements do not align with the Strecker Memorandum's claims. He co-discovered HIV.

keyFigures.name: Anthony Fauci
keyFigures.role: Frequently cited by critics as a central figure in the alleged conspiracy, representing the mainstream medical establishment.

timeline.event: Emergence of the AIDS epidemic and initial scientific investigations.
Robert Strecker develops his theory and publishes the Strecker Memorandum.
The Strecker Memorandum gains traction within conspiracy theory circles.
The theory persists online and is frequently cited in AIDS denialism and anti-vaccine movements.

sources.author: Seth Kalichman
sources.work: Denying AIDS: Conspiracy Theories, Pseudoscience, and Human Tragedy
sources.title: Seth Kalichman
